questions.stem: What is the time complexity of Python's sorted() function?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(n²)
O(log n)
questions.explanation: Python uses Timsort, which is O(n log n) in the worst case and O(n) for already-sorted data. No comparison-based sort can do better than O(n log n) in the general case.

questions.stem: Why must binary search only be used on sorted data?
options: It runs slower on unsorted data
Binary search works by eliminating half the remaining elements each step — this only works if you can reliably determine which half contains the target
Python raises an error on unsorted input
It uses more memory with unsorted data
questions.explanation: Binary search compares the middle element and discards half the array. This only works because in a sorted array, if target > arr[mid], it MUST be in the right half. Unsorted data breaks this guarantee.

questions.stem: What is the difference between `list.sort()` and `sorted(list)`?
options: sorted() is faster
list.sort() modifies the list in place and returns None; sorted() returns a new sorted list
list.sort() is stable; sorted() is not
They are identical in all ways
questions.explanation: list.sort() is in-place (modifies original, returns None). sorted() creates and returns a NEW sorted list, leaving the original unchanged. Both are stable and use Timsort.

questions.stem: When would you use a heap (heapq) instead of sorting the full list?
options: When the list is very short
When you only need the smallest/largest k elements, or need to repeatedly add elements and find the minimum
When the data contains strings
Heaps are always better than sorting
questions.explanation: Sorting takes O(n log n) to get the top-k. A heap takes O(n log k) — much faster when k << n. Heaps also support O(log n) insertion with efficient min-access, which sorting doesn't.

questions.stem: What is the time complexity of binary search?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each step. To search n elements: n → n/2 → n/4 → ... → 1. This takes log₂(n) steps = O(log n). For 1 billion items, that's ~30 comparisons.

questions.stem: How does Python's `sorted()` accept a custom sort key?
options: You write a comparison function returning -1, 0, or 1
Using the `key=` parameter with a function that returns the value to sort by
By passing a sorted comparison class
It only sorts in natural order
questions.explanation: The `key=` parameter accepts any callable. `sorted(students, key=lambda s: s.grade)` sorts by the `.grade` attribute. Python calls `key(item)` once per item and sorts by those results.
